audit_status.review_note: Reviewed verse by verse for major Hebrew wording, grammar, context, and interpretive translation choices. Final scholarly review may still refine individual renderings and textual-variant notes.

verses.restored: Now the serpent was more cunning than every living being of the field that the Eternal Divine had made. And it said to the woman, Did the Divine really say, You shall not eat from any tree of the garden?

familiar.text: Now the serpent was more subtil than any beast of the field which the LORD God had made. And he said unto the woman, Yea, hath God said, Ye shall not eat of every tree of the garden?

notes.title: Serpent, not Satan in Genesis
notes.body: Genesis names the figure as the serpent. Later traditions connect the serpent with Satan, but that identification is not explicit in this chapter.

notes.title: Cunning
notes.body: Arum means crafty, shrewd, or cunning. It wordplays with naked/arummim from the previous verse.

verses.restored: And the woman said to the serpent, From the fruit of the trees of the garden we may eat.

familiar.text: And the woman said unto the serpent, We may eat of the fruit of the trees of the garden:

verses.restored: But from the fruit of the tree that is in the middle of the garden, the Divine said, You shall not eat from it, and you shall not touch it, lest you die.

familiar.text: But of the fruit of the tree which is in the midst of the garden, God hath said, Ye shall not eat of it, neither shall ye touch it, lest ye die.

notes.title: Do not touch
notes.body: The original command in Genesis 2 mentioned not eating; the woman’s speech adds not touching. The text may be showing the command already being expanded in transmission.

verses.restored: And the serpent said to the woman, You will not surely die.

familiar.text: And the serpent said unto the woman, Ye shall not surely die:

notes.title: Direct contradiction
notes.body: The serpent directly reverses the warning. The Hebrew echoes the earlier phrase dying you shall die.

verses.restored: For the Divine knows that in the day you eat from it, your eyes will be opened, and you will be like divine beings, knowing good and harm.

familiar.text: For God doth know that in the day ye eat thereof, then your eyes shall be opened, and ye shall be as gods, knowing good and evil.

notes.title: Divine beings
notes.body: Elohim here may mean God, gods, or divine beings. Because the serpent speaks of becoming like elohim, divine beings preserves the ambiguity.

notes.title: Good and harm
notes.body: Ra can mean evil, bad, harm, trouble, or adversity. Harm is used to avoid importing later abstract moral theology too quickly.

verses.restored: And the woman saw that the tree was good for food, that it was desirable to the eyes, and that the tree was desirable for gaining insight. She took from its fruit and ate; and she also gave to her man with her, and he ate.

familiar.text: And when the woman saw that the tree was good for food, and that it was pleasant to the eyes, and a tree to be desired to make one wise, she took of the fruit thereof, and did eat, and gave also unto her husband with her; and he did eat.

notes.title: Desirable for insight
notes.body: The word points to gaining wisdom, understanding, or insight. The verse is psychologically layered: food, beauty, and wisdom all draw the human will.

notes.title: With her
notes.body: The man is described as with her. The text does not present him as absent from the scene.

verses.restored: Then the eyes of the two of them were opened, and they knew that they were naked. They sewed fig leaves together and made coverings for themselves.

familiar.text: And the eyes of them both were opened, and they knew that they were naked; and they sewed fig leaves together and made themselves aprons.

notes.title: Knowledge becomes self-consciousness
notes.body: The opened eyes first produce awareness of nakedness. The story moves from unashamed embodiment to self-protective covering.

notes.title: Coverings
notes.body: The word can mean loin-coverings or girdles, not full clothing.

verses.restored: And they heard the sound of the Eternal Divine walking in the garden in the wind of the day, and the human and his woman hid themselves from the face of the Eternal Divine among the trees of the garden.

familiar.text: And they heard the voice of the LORD God walking in the garden in the cool of the day: and Adam and his wife hid themselves from the presence of the LORD God amongst the trees of the garden.

notes.title: Sound, not only voice
notes.body: Qol can mean voice, sound, or noise. Sound is used because the verse describes hearing the Divine moving in the garden.

notes.title: Wind of the day
notes.body: The familiar cool of the day is possible, but the Hebrew includes ruach, wind or breath. Wind of the day keeps the word visible.

notes.title: Hiding from the face
notes.body: The Hebrew says from the face/presence. Shame turns presence into something to hide from.

verses.restored: And the Eternal Divine called to the human and said to him, Where are you?

familiar.text: And the LORD God called unto Adam, and said unto him, Where art thou?

notes.title: Where are you?
notes.body: The first divine question after the eating is relational, not informational. It invites the hidden human to come into awareness.

verses.restored: And he said, I heard your sound in the garden, and I was afraid because I was naked, so I hid myself.

familiar.text: And he said, I heard thy voice in the garden, and I was afraid, because I was naked; and I hid myself.

notes.title: Fear enters
notes.body: Fear, nakedness, and hiding now appear together. The issue is not body alone, but consciousness of exposure through fear.

verses.restored: And the Divine said, Who told you that you were naked? Have you eaten from the tree from which I commanded you not to eat?

familiar.text: And he said, Who told thee that thou wast naked? Hast thou eaten of the tree, whereof I commanded thee that thou shouldest not eat?

notes.title: Who told you?
notes.body: The question points to a new inner narration: the human now interprets nakedness through shame.

verses.restored: And the human said, The woman whom you gave to be with me, she gave me from the tree, and I ate.

familiar.text: And the man said, The woman whom thou gavest to be with me, she gave me of the tree, and I did eat.

notes.title: Displacement of responsibility
notes.body: The human admits eating, but shifts responsibility toward the woman and indirectly toward the Divine who gave her.

verses.restored: And the Eternal Divine said to the woman, What is this you have done? And the woman said, The serpent deceived me, and I ate.

familiar.text: And the LORD God said unto the woman, What is this that thou hast done? And the woman said, The serpent beguiled me, and I did eat.

notes.title: Deceived
notes.body: The verb means deceived, misled, or beguiled. The woman names the serpent’s influence, but still says I ate.

verses.restored: And the Eternal Divine said to the serpent, Because you have done this, cursed are you more than every herd animal and every living being of the field. On your belly you shall go, and dust you shall eat all the days of your life.

familiar.text: And the LORD God said unto the serpent, Because thou hast done this, thou art cursed above all cattle, and above every beast of the field; upon thy belly shalt thou go, and dust shalt thou eat all the days of thy life:

notes.title: Serpent cursed
notes.body: In the Hebrew flow, the serpent is cursed. The human and woman receive consequences, but the text does not use the same cursed are you formula for them here.

verses.restored: And I will set enmity between you and the woman, and between your seed and her seed. He will strike your head, and you will strike his heel.

familiar.text: And I will put enmity between thee and the woman, and between thy seed and her seed; it shall bruise thy head, and thou shalt bruise his heel.

notes.title: Seed of the woman
notes.body: Seed is normally lineage or offspring language. Later Christian tradition reads this messianically, but the immediate Hebrew image is conflict between serpent-line and human-line.

notes.title: Strike
notes.body: The same Hebrew verb is used for both head and heel. Strike is used instead of making one bruise and the other crush unless interpretation requires it.

verses.restored: To the woman the Divine said, I will greatly multiply your pain and your conception; in pain you will bear children. Your longing will be toward your man, and he will rule over you.

familiar.text: Unto the woman he said, I will greatly multiply thy sorrow and thy conception; in sorrow thou shalt bring forth children; and thy desire shall be to thy husband, and he shall rule over thee.

notes.title: Pain and conception
notes.body: The Hebrew is compressed and difficult. It may refer to pain in conception, pregnancy, and childbirth, not only the moment of birth.

notes.title: Description, not endorsement
notes.body: He will rule over you describes a broken relational pattern after the rupture. It should not be treated as a divine command that men should dominate women.

notes.title: Longing
notes.body: Teshuqah can mean desire, longing, or turning toward. It recurs in Genesis 4:7, making the phrase complex.

verses.restored: And to the human the Divine said, Because you listened to the voice of your woman and ate from the tree about which I commanded you, saying, You shall not eat from it, cursed is the ground because of you. In painful toil you shall eat from it all the days of your life.

familiar.text: And unto Adam he said, Because thou hast hearkened unto the voice of thy wife, and hast eaten of the tree, of which I commanded thee, saying, Thou shalt not eat of it: cursed is the ground for thy sake; in sorrow shalt thou eat of it all the days of thy life;

notes.title: Ground is cursed, not the human directly
notes.body: The curse formula falls on the ground because of the human. The human’s relationship with the ground becomes painful toil.

notes.title: Listened to the voice
notes.body: This phrase should not be used as a blanket condemnation of listening to women. The issue in context is eating against the command.

verses.restored: Thorns and thistles it will sprout for you, and you shall eat the plants of the field.

familiar.text: Thorns also and thistles shall it bring forth to thee; and thou shalt eat the herb of the field;

notes.title: Resistance from the ground
notes.body: The ground still produces food, but now also resistance. The relationship between human labor and earth is altered.

verses.restored: By the sweat of your face you shall eat bread, until you return to the ground, for from it you were taken. For dust you are, and to dust you shall return.

familiar.text: In the sweat of thy face shalt thou eat bread, till thou return unto the ground; for out of it wast thou taken: for dust thou art, and unto dust shalt thou return.

notes.title: Mortality
notes.body: The human is reminded of earth-origin and earth-return. This is mortality language, not eternal torture language.

notes.title: Dust to dust
notes.body: The phrase closes the loop opened in Genesis 2:7: human from dust, returning to dust.

verses.restored: And the human called his woman’s name Eve, because she was mother of all living.

familiar.text: And Adam called his wife's name Eve; because she was the mother of all living.

notes.title: Eve/Havvah
notes.body: The name is linked with life/living. The naming follows the mortality sentence, placing life-bearing in direct contrast with death.

verses.restored: And the Eternal Divine made garments of skin for the human and his woman, and clothed them.

familiar.text: Unto Adam also and to his wife did the LORD God make coats of skins, and clothed them.

notes.title: Clothed by the Divine
notes.body: Even after rupture, the Divine provides covering. The text moves from human-made fig coverings to divinely made garments.

verses.restored: And the Eternal Divine said, Look, the human has become like one of us, knowing good and harm. And now, lest he stretch out his hand and also take from the tree of life, and eat, and live forever—

familiar.text: And the LORD God said, Behold, the man is become as one of us, to know good and evil: and now, lest he put forth his hand, and take also of the tree of life, and eat, and live for ever:

notes.title: Like one of us
notes.body: The phrase preserves the mysterious plural speech already seen in Genesis 1:26.

notes.title: Life forever blocked
notes.body: The concern is not knowledge alone, but knowledge joined with unending life in the ruptured state.

verses.restored: therefore the Eternal Divine sent the human out from the garden of Eden, to serve the ground from which the human had been taken.

familiar.text: Therefore the LORD God sent him forth from the garden of Eden, to till the ground from whence he was taken.

notes.title: Sent out to serve the ground
notes.body: The human returns to the ground relationship, but now outside the garden. Serve is again used for avad.

verses.restored: So the Divine drove out the human, and stationed east of the garden of Eden the cherubim and the flaming, turning sword, to guard the way to the tree of life.

familiar.text: So he drove out the man; and he placed at the east of the garden of Eden Cherubims, and a flaming sword which turned every way, to keep the way of the tree of life.

notes.title: Guarding the way
notes.body: The way to the tree of life is guarded, not erased. The story leaves a guarded path rather than pure annihilation.

notes.title: Cherubim
notes.body: Cherubim are not chubby winged babies. In ancient imagery they are powerful throne or guardian beings.
